title: Shri Guru-vandanā (Muni Vandanā) Sūtra
summary: This sūtra is a salutation to Jain monks. In its first part the reciter bows, with head, mind and forehead, to every true muni anywhere in the inhabited universe — to all who carry the marks of the ascetic (the woollen broom, the cloth-tuft and the alms-bowl), keep the five great vows, and hold the eighteen-thousand-fold code of pure conduct. Its second part then honours the reciter's own teacher-line: the Anchalgachchha (Vidhipaksha-gaccha) paṭṭāvalī, the unbroken succession of head-teachers from Gaṇadhara Sudharmāsvāmī down through Āryarakṣitasūri to the later Sāgara-named ācāryas. It is recited so that veneration of the monkhood and gratitude to one's lineage of gurus become part of the daily reflection.
ritualPlacement: Recited in the Daivasika (Devasi) pratikramaṇa only, in the closing study-portion: after the fourth Āvaśyaka (the short confession, Laghu Atiyār) and the Dvādaśāvarta Vāndaṇā, and before the sajjhāy cluster (Drumapuṣpikā, Pañca-Parameṣṭhi, Mannaha Jiṇāṇaṁ). In role it is a sajjhāy-style veneration, not one of the six Āvaśyakas. Unlike the core Āvaśyaka sūtras it is not carried over with a word-substitution into Pakkhī/Cāumāsī/Sāṁvatsarī; it belongs to the Devasi sequence. Its second half is gaccha-specific: the paṭṭāvalī names the Anchalgachchha line, so other gacchas recite a different succession here.
authorAndSource: Two strata. (1) The opening salutation (Aḍhāijjesu dīva-samuddesu… te savve sirasā maṇasā matthaeṇa vandāmi) is a traditional Prakrit gāthā-pair shared widely across the Śvetāmbara pratikramaṇa liturgy; it is anonymous/received, not authored by this gaccha. (2) The paṭṭāvalī portion is a tradition-maintained succession-litany of the Anchalgachchha (Vidhipaksha-gaccha); it is composite and was extended over time as the lineage grew, here running to Vidyāsāgarasūri and Udayasāgarasūri. No single named author is attested for the litany; treat its redaction as traditional. Verified against the Anchalgachchha pattāvalī tradition, which counts Āryarakṣitasūri as the 47th paṭṭa from Sudharmāsvāmī.
structureArc: Opening salutation, gāthās (1)–(2): a universal homage — (1) bows to every muni throughout the two-and-a-half continents (aḍhāī dvīpa) and the fifteen karmabhūmis who bear the ascetic's emblems (rajoharaṇa, gucchha, paḍiggaha); (2) extends the homage to keepers of the five great vows and the eighteen-thousand-fold śīlāṅga, of unbroken pure conduct, saluted with head, mind and forehead. Then the paṭṭāvalī: an opening prose roll names the first eight seats (Sudharmā → Jambū → Prabhava → Sijjambhava → Yaśobhadra → Sambhūtivijaya → Bhadrabāhu → Sthūlibhadra), then leaps 'paṭṭānupāṭṭa' to the forty-seventh paṭṭadhara, Āryarakṣitasūri, 'ornament of the Vidhipaksha-gaccha,' the jaṅgama yugapradhāna. Paṭṭāvalī gāthās (1)–(6) then carry the chain forward seat by seat: (1) Jayasiṁha, Dharmaghoṣa, Mahendrasiṁha; (2) Siṁhaprabha, Ajayasiṁha, Devendrasiṁha, Dharmaprabha; (3) Siṁhatilaka, Mahendraprabha, Merutuṅga, Jayakīrti; (4) Jayakesarī, Siddhāntasāgara, Bhāvasāgara, Guṇanidhāna; (5) Dharmamūrti, Kalyāṇasāgara, Amarasāgara — closing with the benediction 'may he work the welfare of the saṅgha'; (6) Vidyāsāgara and Udayasāgara, ending in further homage.
recurringImagery: The governing motif of the paṭṭāvalī is the paṭṭa — the 'seat' or throne of pontifical succession — and the refrain tappaṭṭe / tappaya ('on his seat,' 'after him on the seat'), which threads ācārya to ācārya as an unbroken chain of authority back to the Gaṇadhara. Read each name not as an isolated saint but as one link whose legitimacy is its place in this continuous transmission; the litany's force is cumulative, the living lineage (jaṅgama) standing behind the present reciter. In the salutation half, the recurring marks rajoharaṇa, gucchha and paḍiggaha function as a shorthand: wherever these emblems are found, a true muni is found and is to be honoured — the homage is to the ascetic ideal, not to any one person.
keyThemes: the universal scope of homage — every true ascetic, everywhere
the emblems and vows that define a muni
gratitude to one's own gaccha and gurus

glossary.definition: A Jain monk; a renunciant who has taken the great vows.
An ascetic/monk; in the salutation, 'all the sādhus' wherever they may be.
Reverential salutation; here the act of bowing to munis and gurus (vandāmi, 'I bow').
The woollen broom an ascetic uses to gently sweep a place before sitting, to avoid harming tiny beings; an emblem of the monk.
A cloth-tuft/whisk among the monastic implements (associated with the cloth coverings of the alms-vessels); listed as a mark of the ascetic.
The alms-bowl (pātra) into which a monk receives food; one of the defining ascetic possessions.
The five great vows of a monk: non-violence, truth, non-stealing, celibacy and non-possession.
A 'limb of conduct'; the tradition enumerates aṣṭādaśa-sahasra (18,000) such components of perfect monastic conduct, here invoked as 'aḍhāra sahassa sīlaṅga.'
The 'two-and-a-half continents' (Jambūdvīpa, Dhātakīkhaṇḍa and half of Puṣkaravara) with their intervening oceans — the whole region of the human/inhabited world.
A 'land of action' where ascetic life and liberation are possible; the fifteen karmabhūmis (5 Bharata, 5 Airāvata, 5 Mahāvideha) bound the scope of the salutation.
The 'seat' of pontifical succession; the throne passed from one ācārya to the next (the ordinal 'pahele pāṭe…' = 'on the first seat…').
A succession-list of the heads of a lineage, recited to honour the unbroken transmission of teaching.
'Seat after seat'; in continuous succession, the phrase that links the early paṭṭas to the later ones.
The pre-eminent spiritual leader of an age; a title given here to Āryarakṣitasūri.
The 'pure-procedure' order — this tradition's own name for the Anchalgachchha (Achalgachchha).
Honorific for an ācārya/preceptor (suffixed to lineage names, e.g. Āryarakṣita-sūri).

howToRead: A newcomer can read the first two gāthās as a single warm bow to all monks everywhere, then let the names that follow wash past as a roll of honour — the point is the unbroken chain, not memorising each name. An advanced practitioner should track the tappaṭṭe/tappaya refrain to follow the exact Anchalgachchha succession from Sudharmāsvāmī through the 47th paṭṭa Āryarakṣitasūri down to the Sāgara-line ācāryas, noting the closing benediction for the welfare of the saṅgha.

sources.title: Achal Gaccha (Anchalgachchha) — overview and pattavali
sources.consultedFor: Vidhipaksha-gaccha identity; Āryarakṣitasūri as founder-reformer counted 47th in succession from Sudharmāsvāmī.

sources.title: Āryarakṣitasūri — book summary (JainGPT)
sources.consultedFor: Āryarakṣitasūri's place in the paṭṭāvalī and the early succession names (Sudharmā, Jambū, Prabhava…).

notes: Renderings of gucchha and paḍiggaha follow standard Śvetāmbara usage (cloth-tuft/whisk and alms-bowl); the precise referent of gucchha varies slightly across commentaries and is glossed conservatively. The '18,000 śīlāṅga' is the traditional enumerated total of components of monastic conduct; the text invokes the figure without itemising it. Per the authoritative source book, spellings are preserved verbatim (e.g. the Prakrit forms of names, doubled/single conjuncts, and mātrās), and may differ from the IAST glosses used here for clarity. The paṭṭāvalī is gaccha-specific and tradition-extended; lineage spans beyond Udayasāgarasūri exist in later recensions but are not part of this sūtra's text as given. Verified lineage facts are limited to the founder/47th-paṭṭa claim; intermediate names rest on the book and the gaccha's own paṭṭāvalī rather than independent dated attestation.
